refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods. Compare against ≤1% threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on control fidelity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds the acceptable threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission protocols tuned to network conditions to recover lost packets.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: use FEC for latency-tolerant telemetry and QoS-protected retransmission for latency-critical control commands.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining adaptive forward error correction (FEC) for telemetry streams and QoS-protected selective retransmission for control commands, with real-time packet loss monitoring and operator alerting.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation control logic (command encoding/decoding) with network transport layer (FEC, QoS, retransmission). This is acceptable because control fidelity is inherently dependent on communication reliability.
DP-D couples safety alerting (operator notification of packet loss threshold breach) with teleoperation monitoring. This is necessary and acceptable given high safety criticality.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids unnecessary coupling to vehicle propulsion or sensor acquisition logic. FEC and QoS are localized to the communication subsystem. Coupling is minimal and justified by functional necessity.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment scenarios. Risk: If actual field conditions differ significantly, packet loss may exceed threshold despite DP implementation.

Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth expansion) is acceptable within available network capacity. Risk: If network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase effective packet loss by consuming capacity.
Assumption: Operator can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts. Risk: If operator is distracted or alert fatigue occurs, safety benefit of alerting is diminished.
Assumption: Retransmission latency is tolerable for control commands. Risk: If network round-trip time is high, retransmission may introduce unacceptable control lag.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC code rate and algorithm selection criteria based on network latency profile; (2) retransmission timeout and retry limits for control commands; (3) packet loss threshold for operator alert (recommend: 0.5% as early warning, 1% as critical threshold); (4) fallback behavior if packet loss cannot be controlled (e.g., autonomous safe stop).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote operation, including command encoding/decoding, FEC implementation, QoS enforcement, retransmission logic, packet loss monitoring, and operator alerting. Interfaces with Vehicle for command delivery and with Safety Services for alert escalation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from remote operator station. Tasking carries control intent that must be protected by FEC and QoS.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and operational status back to operator. Telemetry is protected by FEC to ensure operator situational awareness despite packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates packet loss threshold breach events to Safety Services for operator notification and potential autonomous safety actions (e.g., safe stop).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (cellular, satellite, or hybrid). Teleoperation module monitors Network Link quality metrics and adapts FEC/QoS parameters dynamically.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, QoS, retransmission, and monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert escalation crosses module boundary (acceptable and necessary).

Vehicle module should not own communication protocol logic; it should focus on actuation and sensor acquisition. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter communication concerns across vehicle firmware, creating high diffusion and maintenance burden.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting, but not the underlying communication reliability mechanisms. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication engineering with safety policy, reducing modularity.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for DP-D ownership. Assigning to NGHE Solution would bypass the Teleoperation module abstraction and create diffusion across multiple subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because: (1) FEC, QoS, and retransmission are intrinsic to teleoperation communication; (2) packet loss monitoring is a teleoperation-specific concern; (3) operator alerting is a teleoperation user-facing function; (4) containment score of 95 reflects minimal diffusion and high cohesion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (FEC implementation + QoS logic + retransmission + monitoring + alerting), it may violate single-responsibility principle. Mitigation: Decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., CommunicationReliability, PacketLossMonitor, OperatorAlert) at the next decomposition level.

Risk: Network Link interface may be unstable or poorly defined, making it difficult for Teleoperation to adapt FEC/QoS parameters. Mitigation: Establish clear Network Link metrics contract (latency, jitter, bandwidth, packet loss rate) in interface specification.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation may create tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: Use event-driven or publish-subscribe pattern to decouple; Safety Services subscribes to packet loss threshold events without Teleoperation knowing the subscriber.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has clear ownership of Network Link interface and can query/adapt to network conditions dynamically. Establish explicit contract for SafetyAlert escalation (threshold values, alert frequency, escalation priority). Consider decomposing Teleoperation into sub-modules if complexity exceeds team capacity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid FEC + QoS + retransmission + monitoring) directly addresses FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤1%. FEC recovers lost packets in telemetry; QoS protects control commands; monitoring enables real-time detection; alerting enables operator response. No logical gap between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of communication reliability logic. FEC, QoS, and retransmission are localized to Teleoperation; no scattering across Vehicle or Safety Services.
Acceptable coupling: SafetyAlert escalation is necessary and minimal; it represents a clean interface boundary rather than diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. All core logic (FEC, QoS, retransmission, monitoring) is contained within Teleoperation. Only SafetyAlert escalation crosses boundary, which is intentional and well-defined.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: packet loss can be measured end-to-end across Teleoperation module interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out). Test harness can inject network faults and measure FEC/QoS effectiveness. Acceptance criterion (≤1%) is quantifiable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≤1% packet loss under agreed network conditions. Vehicle module is responsible for executing commands received via Teleoperation. Safety Services is responsible for responding to SafetyAlert escalations. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit network condition profile (latency, jitter, bandwidth, baseline packet loss) agreed with customer. This profile drives FEC code rate and QoS parameter selection.
Define packet loss threshold alert levels: (1) warning threshold (0.5%), (2) critical threshold (1%), (3) failure threshold (>1%). Specify operator actions for each level.
Specify fallback behavior if packet loss cannot be controlled: autonomous safe stop, operator override, or graceful degradation. This is a safety-critical decision that must be made at this level.

Confirm that Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources (CPU, memory) to implement FEC and QoS logic without impacting control latency. If not, recommend hardware acceleration or algorithm optimization.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation). DP-D is sound and well-justified, but requires further decomposition to address each sub-FR explicitly. Teleoperation module assignment is correct and well-contained. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify implementation details (FEC algorithm selection, QoS policy, alert thresholds, fallback logic) and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (target: detection latency <100ms).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement forward error correction (FEC) for telemetry streams and selective retransmission for control commands to minimize effective packet loss (target: effective packet loss <0.1% after FEC/retransmission).

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall alert the operator and escalate to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds 0.5% (warning) or 1% (critical), and shall execute autonomous safe stop if packet loss exceeds 2% for >5 seconds.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of deployment scenarios.
FEC overhead (bandwidth expansion) is acceptable within available network capacity.
Operator can respond appropriately to packet loss alerts and will not experience alert fatigue.
Retransmission latency is tolerable for control commands (assumed <200ms round-trip time).
Teleoperation module has sufficient computational resources to implement FEC and QoS without impacting control latency.
SafetyAlert escalation mechanism is available and functional in Safety Services module.
open_risks: Risk: Actual field network conditions may differ from customer-agreed profile, causing packet loss to exceed threshold despite DP implementation. Mitigation: Conduct field trials with representative network conditions; implement adaptive FEC/QoS tuning.
Risk: FEC overhead may consume network capacity, paradoxically increasing effective packet loss. Mitigation: Conduct capacity analysis; select FEC code rate based on network bandwidth profile.
Risk: Operator alert fatigue may reduce safety effectiveness. Mitigation: Implement intelligent alert filtering; escalate to autonomous safe stop if operator does not respond to critical alerts.

Risk: Retransmission latency may introduce unacceptable control lag. Mitigation: Measure control latency under FEC/retransmission; implement latency budgeting; consider predictive control or operator training to compensate.
Risk: Teleoperation module may become too large and complex. Mitigation: Decompose into sub-modules (CommunicationReliability, PacketLossMonitor, OperatorAlert) at next level.
evidence_needed: Customer-agreed network condition profile (latency, jitter, bandwidth, baseline packet loss).
FEC algorithm selection and code rate justification based on network profile.
QoS policy specification (priority levels, traffic shaping rules, bandwidth allocation).
Packet loss threshold alert levels and operator action procedures.
Fallback behavior specification (autonomous safe stop criteria and execution logic).
Control latency budget and FEC/retransmission impact analysis.
Computational resource availability and FEC/QoS implementation feasibility study.
SafetyAlert escalation mechanism specification and integration plan with Safety Services.
